risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Block randomisation of nursing homes will use a computer generated allocation sequence and occur after initial consent. Randomisation will be stratified by organisational affiliation ... [and] rurality and dementia-specificity" Comment: This clearly describes a proper random sequence generation method (computer-generated allocation sequence with block and stratified randomisation), so the allocation was truly random.

Quote: "Block randomisation of nursing homes will use a computer generated allocation sequence and occur after initial consent." Comment: This report does not explicitly state a central service, SNOSE, or other named concealment method, but because randomisation was at cluster level and conducted by a blinded statistician ("The statistician (GL) will be blinded to nursing home identity for randomisation and analysis"), the recruiting nursing homes/managers would not have been able to foresee the upcoming allocation before recruitment of each cluster; this is the clearest available evidence consistent with concealed allocation in this trial.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The pragmatic nature of the trial and complex nature of the intervention requires careful consideration of the feasibility versus desirability of blinding... nursing home managers will not be blinded to allocation... Nursing staff and families at nursing homes in the intervention arm will also know that facilitated case conferencing is being implemented because they will be participating." Comment: Participants and key personnel were not blinded, and the intervention is behaviorally complex with outcomes (e.g., family-rated and nurse-rated end-of-life care, staff attitudes, quality of life) plausibly influenced by knowledge of allocation and participation, so performance bias is likely.

Quote: "Research assistants collecting process and outcome data will also be blinded to the specific design and aims of the study, and each assistant will visit nursing homes in either the intervention or usual care arm only." Comment: Outcome assessment was performed by research assistants who were blinded to the study's design/aims, which reduces the likelihood that allocation knowledge influenced measurement. The main outcomes were family-rated EOLD scales collected by interview 4 to 6 weeks after death, and nurse-rated proxy outcomes; these assessments could be vulnerable to bias, but the explicit blinding of the outcome-data collectors makes detection bias unlikely.

Quote: "Primary and secondary analyses will be performed on an intention to treat (ITT) basis." Comment: The paper describes an ITT analysis with mixed/multilevel models, and the protocol anticipates only modest resident dropout ("anticipated dropout rate at the resident level of 10 %"). The methods do not report any concerning imbalance or outcome-related attrition that would suggest bias in incomplete outcome data.
